cl_2023_cdc_10_q_1.instructions:
Directions for questions 1 to 4: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

Equal opportunity, also called equality of opportunity, in political theory is the idea that people ought to be able to compete on equal terms, or on a “level playing field,” for advantaged offices and positions. Proponents of equal opportunity believe that the principle is compatible with, and indeed may justify, inequalities of outcome of some sort. . .

The ideal of equal opportunity does not necessarily lead to equality of outcome since its aim is consistent with allowing people’s life prospects to be influenced by their values and choices. From that standpoint, the underlying motivation of the ideal of equal opportunity, properly understood, is to counteract the effects of people’s different natural and social circumstances while permitting inequalities of condition that emerge as a result of their choices. On that basis, some scholars have argued that inequalities arising from differences in choice are not only just but necessary, to give personal responsibility its due. That view is sometimes described as luck egalitarianism.

Luck egalitarianism maintains that, while inequalities are unjust if they derive from differences in people’s circumstances—because circumstances are a matter of brute luck—they are just if they are the product of people’s voluntary choices. Luck egalitarianism is thus a combination of two different claims: first, that justice requires the neutralization of the effects of differences in people’s circumstances, and, second, that it is just to require people to bear the costs, or allow them to enjoy the benefits, of their voluntary choices. In making those claims, luck egalitarianism invokes a distinction between choice and circumstance, or between brute luck and “option luck.”

Luck egalitarianism has its critics, however. Given the social forces to which each person is subject, the distinction between choice and circumstance, or between brute luck and option luck, is not always easy to draw in a plausible way. But even if a satisfactory way of drawing those distinctions could be found, there is still the worry that luck egalitarianism is too harsh in the way that it holds people responsible for their foolish or reckless behaviour. It seems to imply that those who end up needy as a result of their own imprudence can justly be forced to bear the costs of their choices. So, people who choose to smoke with full knowledge of the risks involved and develop lung cancer may have no entitlement to the health care that they need but cannot afford. Uncompromising luck egalitarians may insist that they have no objection to voluntary schemes to help those with self-inflicted needs but that they regard the forcible extraction of taxes to help those who are responsible for their plight as sanctioning the exploitation of the prudent. Others, however, may concede that luck egalitarianism should be supplemented with a further principle of justice, such as, for example, a principle holding that the needy—that is, those whose condition falls below some threshold—are entitled to support regardless of how their needs arose.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_1.question_text: Each one of the following inequalities would be considered just by luck egalitarianism EXCEPT: 1   entrepreneurs who invest more time and effort into their ventures earn a higher profit than those who don't in the same market conditions. 2 professional athletes who consistently train harder and perform better secure more endorsements than their less committed peers.
options: authors who spend years researching and writing quality books receive more royalties compared to those who quickly produce low-quality work.
students who attend extra classes by choice due to opportunity and score better in exams get admission to top-tier universities, while others don't.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_1.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Option (4), which pertains to students attending extra classes due to opportunity and subsequently performing better in exams, is the one not consistent with luck egalitarianism. The principle of luck egalitarianism asserts that while outcomes resulting from one's choices are just, outcomes resulting from circumstances (or brute luck) are unjust. Here, while attending extra classes and working hard might be seen as a choice, the availability of the "opportunity" to attend these classes might be due to circumstances beyond a student's control. Therefore, the resulting inequality in admissions based on this mixed scenario of choice and circumstance runs counter to the ethos of luck egalitarianism.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) Entrepreneurs who invest more time and effort into their ventures earn a higher profit than those who don't in the same market conditions. This situation aligns with luck egalitarianism as the differences in profit are a direct outcome of individual choices. Entrepreneurs who voluntarily choose to invest more time and effort are rewarded for their decisions. This inequality is based on choice, not circumstance, and is thus consistent with luck egalitarianism.

(2) Professional athletes securing more endorsements due to their consistent training and performance resonates with the choice-driven premise of luck egalitarianism. Athletes make the voluntary choice to train harder and, as a result, perform better. Their success in securing more endorsements is a direct result of their efforts and choices, fitting perfectly within the framework of luck egalitarianism.

(3) Authors earning more royalties due to extensive research and quality work reflects the principle of outcomes based on choices. An author's decision to spend years on research and writing, and the subsequent royalties they earn, is a product of their choices. Such an inequality, based on the quality of effort and choice, aligns with the views of luck egalitarianism.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_2.question_text: Each of the following is a criticism of luck egalitarianism that the passage suggests EXCEPT:
options: luck egalitarianism, on its own, may lack a comprehensive approach to justice.
luck egalitarianism is too harsh when it comes to the self-inflicted needs of individuals.
strict adherence to luck egalitarianism can lead to a lack of solutions to option luck.
it's challenging to clearly differentiate between what is a choice and what is a mere circumstance.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_2.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Option (3), "strict adherence to luck egalitarianism can lead to a lack of solutions to option luck," is not a criticism that the passage explicitly or implicitly suggests. While the passage discusses the principles of luck egalitarianism and the distinction between brute luck and option luck, it does not indicate any potential shortcomings of luck egalitarianism in addressing or providing solutions for situations governed by option luck.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) The passage hints that luck egalitarianism might not be sufficient as a stand-alone principle for justice. This is inferred from the section discussing the potential need to supplement luck egalitarianism with another principle of justice. Specifically, there's a mention that some people believe there should be a principle ensuring support for the needy, irrespective of the reasons for their condition. This implies that luck egalitarianism, on its own, might not comprehensively cover all facets of justice, especially when considering the complexities of real-life situations.

(2) The passage directly articulates this criticism. It brings forth a moral dilemma that arises from the strict adherence to luck egalitarianism. The text raises concerns about individuals who, due to their imprudent choices (like knowingly taking up smoking), end up in dire circumstances. Strict luck egalitarianism seems to imply that such individuals might justly bear the full consequences of their decisions without societal assistance. This strict stance, especially in cases like healthcare needs, showcases the potential harshness of luck egalitarianism.

(4) The passage emphasizes the practical challenges associated with determining what qualifies as a choice and what is simply a result of circumstance. It is stressed that, given the myriad of social forces each individual is subject to, drawing a clear line between choice (option luck) and circumstance (brute luck) is intricate and often not straightforward. This inherent difficulty could pose challenges in the real-world application of luck egalitarianism, making it a significant criticism.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_3.question_text: Which one of the following, if true, most support the principle of luck egalitarianism?
options: Philosophies rooted in personal choice tend to disregard the importance of societal structures in shaping outcomes, thereby undermining justice.
Distinguishing between factors within one's control and those outside it can highlight foundational tenets of justice-oriented philosophies.
When a philosophy emphasizes personal agency, it typically implies that a person necessarily exhibits behaviours most aligned with their choices.
Philosophical systems prioritizing personal responsibility often aim, but not always succeed, in producing uniform societal outcomes.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_3.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Option (2) supports the principle of luck egalitarianism by highlighting the importance of distinguishing between factors within one's control and those outside it. Luck egalitarianism is grounded in the idea that individuals should be held responsible for their voluntary choices while acknowledging that circumstances beyond their control (brute luck) should not determine their life outcomes. This distinction is a foundational tenet of luck egalitarianism. By recognizing this distinction, society can better address inequalities arising from factors that individuals can control, such as their choices, and intervene to mitigate the impact of circumstances that are beyond their control. In essence, this option aligns with the core principles of luck egalitarianism, emphasizing the need to consider both choices and circumstances when assessing justice in society.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) Option (1) does not directly support the principle of luck egalitarianism. It suggests that philosophies rooted in personal choice tend to disregard the importance of societal structures in shaping outcomes, undermining justice. While it highlights a potential limitation of some philosophies, it does not specifically address the core concept of luck egalitarianism, which is the distinction between choices and circumstances in determining justice.

(3) Option (3) discusses the emphasis on personal agency in philosophy and its alignment with behaviour based on choices. While personal agency is related to individual choices, this option does not directly support luck egalitarianism's key idea of distinguishing between factors within one's control (choices) and those outside of it (circumstances). It focuses more on behaviour than the justice-oriented principles of luck egalitarianism.

(4) Option (4) talks about philosophical systems prioritizing personal responsibility and their aims regarding societal outcomes. It does touch on personal responsibility, which is a concept within luck egalitarianism. However, it also mentions the variability in achieving uniform societal outcomes, which is not a central point in luck egalitarianism. Luck egalitarianism is primarily concerned with the distinction between choices and circumstances rather than the uniformity of societal outcomes, making this option less directly aligned with luck egalitarianism.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_4.question_text: Each of the following is a premise of luck egalitarianism EXCEPT:
options: everyone should have equal compensation to advantaged positions, regardless of their backgrounds.
outcomes, resulting from choices and circumstances, should not be treated the same in a just society.
equal opportunity advocates for an equal start, but it doesn't necessarily mean everyone will have an equal finish.
those who face negative outcomes due to their own choices shouldn't necessarily be bailed out by society.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_4.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Option (1) is not a premise of luck egalitarianism because it suggests that everyone should have equal compensation for advantaged positions, regardless of their backgrounds. Luck egalitarianism is centred on the idea that individuals should be held responsible for their voluntary choices while acknowledging that circumstances beyond their control (brute luck) should not determine their life outcomes. It does not advocate for equal compensation solely based on positions, as it prioritizes personal responsibility and distinguishes between factors within one's control (choices) and those outside of it (circumstances).

Incorrect Answers:
(2) Option (2) aligns with luck egalitarianism. Luck egalitarianism emphasizes that outcomes resulting from choices and circumstances should not be treated the same in a just society. It recognizes the importance of distinguishing between factors within an individual's control (choices) and those beyond their control (circumstances) when assessing justice. This distinction is a key element of luck egalitarian thought, ensuring that individuals are held accountable for their choices while mitigating the effects of factors they cannot control.

(3) Option (3) is also in line with luck egalitarianism. It acknowledges that equal opportunity advocates for an equal start but recognizes that not everyone will necessarily have an equal finish due to differences in choices and circumstances. This premise aligns with the core principles of luck egalitarianism, emphasizing equal opportunity at the outset while acknowledging the role of individual choices and circumstances in determining life outcomes.

(4) Option (4) supports the principle of luck egalitarianism. Luck egalitarianism argues that individuals who face negative outcomes due to their own choices should not necessarily be bailed out by society, as they are responsible for their choices. This notion aligns closely with the idea of personal responsibility, which is a central concept within luck egalitarian thought. It emphasizes that individuals should bear the consequences of their voluntary decisions, contributing to a just society according to luck egalitarian principles.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_5.instructions:
Directions for questions 5 to 8: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

Modern biological warfare began during World War I. Although the Germans refused to use biological agents against people, they did use them against animals, infecting Allied horses with glanders (Burkholderia mallei) and anthrax. . . . During World War II, the infamous Japanese Unit 731 experimentally infected Chinese prisoners of war with horrifying diseases, such as cholera, epidemic hemorrhagic fever, and venereal disease. . . . After World War II, particularly during the Korean War, the United States ratcheted up its biological weapons program. Perhaps the most controversial aspect of the program was the purposeful release of biological agents, such as the relatively harmless Serratia marcescens, over American cities to study weapons dispersal. The military unintentionally infected 11 civilians, one of whom died. By 1969, the U.S. had weaponised anthrax and tularemia. However, in 1975, the U.S. renounced all biological weapons by signing the Biological Weapons Convention (BWC). . . .

Today, biological warfare is feared less from nations and more from terrorist groups or “lone wolves.” But there is disagreement over just how much of a threat this poses. Many believe that terrorists would be incapable of carrying out an effective, large-scale biological attack. . . . The 2001 U.S. anthrax attack killed only 5 people. Sceptics point to incidents like these as evidence that bioterrorists are incapable of inflicting widespread damage. Other analysts disagree. Some biological agents, such as anthrax, require little expertise to grow or weaponise. With microbiological information universally available on the Internet, some experts believe that it is just a matter of time before a large bioterrorist attack occurs. . . .

During the Vietnam War, the Viet-Cong guerillas dug camouflaged pits as booby traps. Inside, they often positioned sharpened bamboo stakes or splinters smeared with human waste. Although it was possible to contract a nasty infection from these, the main purpose was psychological. The tactic worked. The response of American troops was to alter their movements in a way that was disproportionate to the actual threat. An analogous scenario played out following the 2001 anthrax attack in the United States in which there was a colossal disruption of postal services and massive new expenses. Yet, only 5 people died in the attack. (Compare that to the roughly 62,000 Americans who died from influenza and pneumonia that same year.)

Both of these examples serve to underscore two important points: First, biological warfare will almost certainly have a far greater psychological impact than direct impact; and second, protective measures against biological attacks are costly and inconvenient. For instance, giving soldiers vaccines against all possible biological agents would be impractical and possibly dangerous if they have been developed under emergency conditions without thorough testing. Also, vaccines have side effects. . . . Is it worth vaccinating an entire army or nation—knowing ahead of time that many will die or become sick—to protect them against an unlikely threat? From an epidemiological standpoint, the answer is clearly no, which explains why citizens do not receive smallpox vaccinations. The general rule in public health is to vaccinate only if the risk of the disease is greater than the risk of vaccination.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_5.question_text: Which one of the following can be logically inferred from the passage?
options: Biological warfare can lead to long-term psychological trauma in communities.
The direct impact of a bioattack can lead to overreactions and disruptions in daily life.
The fear created by biological attacks in itself can lead to a disproportionate response.
The threat of bioattack can be mitigated if the acceptance of vaccination is high among people.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_5.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
The passage provides concrete examples of the psychological impacts of bioattacks, which in turn lead to disproportionate responses. For instance, the Viet-Cong guerillas during the Vietnam War set up booby traps that, while potentially harmful, were primarily aimed at instilling fear. The fear was so effective that the American troops altered their movements in ways that were not proportional to the actual physical threat of the traps. Similarly, the 2001 anthrax attack in the United States caused a massive disruption in postal services, even though only five people died. This significant reaction to a relatively low fatality event underscores the idea that the fear from biological attacks can lead to oversized responses.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) The passage certainly touches on the psychological effects of biological warfare, highlighting the fear and change in behaviour it can induce. However, there's no specific mention of "long-term psychological trauma." The examples provided (like the Viet-Cong booby traps) show immediate behavioural changes, but we cannot infer from the passage the duration of these psychological impacts.

(2) The passage distinctly contrasts the psychological impact of a bioattack with its direct impact. It emphasizes that the psychological repercussions are typically greater than the direct consequences of the attack. Using the line "First, biological warfare will almost certainly have a far greater psychological impact than direct impact", the passage clearly suggests that it's not the immediate or direct effects of the attack causing the overreactions but rather the fear or psychological aspect. Therefore, while bioattacks can indeed disrupt daily life, as seen with the anthrax attack's impact on postal services, the passage implies that such disruptions are fueled more by the psychological ramifications and not by the direct effects of the attack itself. Hence, option (2) cannot be inferred directly from the text.

(4) While vaccinations are discussed in the passage, it's in the context of the risks and benefits they pose. The passage delves into the decision-making process about when it's worth vaccinating a population, weighing the risks of the disease against the risks of vaccination. The passage does raise concerns about vaccinating under emergency conditions without thorough testing. However, there is no direct link made between high vaccination acceptance and the mitigation of bioattacks.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_6.question_text: The author would most likely agree with which of the following regarding large-scale biological attacks?
options: Given the amount of information about it that is available today, it is more than likely to materialise.
Given the lack of effectiveness of such an attack, it is improbable to manifest in reality.
Given the extent of the damage because of its immediate effect, it is likely to be devastating.
Given the irrationality of how we respond to it, its consequence will likely be significant.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_6.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Option (4) reflects the core sentiment expressed in the passage, specifically highlighting the disproportionate and potentially irrational response to biological threats. The author provides examples, such as the anthrax attack and the Viet-Cong guerillas' tactics, to emphasize that the psychological or fear-driven reaction often outpaces the direct harm caused by the attack. This leads to substantial consequences, even if the immediate physical effects of the attack are relatively minor.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) While the passage does acknowledge that with "microbiological information universally available on the Internet, some experts believe that it is just a matter of time before a large bioterrorist attack occurs," it does not state that it is "more than likely to materialise." The author presents this as an opinion held by some experts, not a definitive conclusion.

(2) The passage does mention that "many believe that terrorists would be incapable of carrying out an effective, large-scale biological attack." However, it also presents a counter-argument suggesting that certain biological agents, like anthrax, are easy to weaponize. The author doesn't definitively side with the idea that it's improbable due to ineffectiveness.

(3) This statement is not entirely aligned with the passage's sentiment. While the passage does not deny the potential devastation of a biological attack, it emphasizes the psychological effects and fear-driven responses more than the direct, immediate impact of such attacks. The examples provided in the passage show that the actual fatalities and direct harm of previous attacks were often lower than the fear and subsequent reactions they generated.

Sceptics point to incidents like these as evidence that bioterrorists are incapable of inflicting widespread damage. Other analysts disagree. Some biological agents, such as anthrax, require little expertise to grow or weaponise. With microbiological information universally available on the Internet, some experts believe that it is just a matter of time before a large bioterrorist attack occurs. . . .

During the Vietnam War, the Viet-Cong guerillas dug camouflaged pits as booby traps. Inside, they often positioned sharpened bamboo stakes or splinters smeared with human waste. Although it was possible to contract a nasty infection from these, the main purpose was psychological. The tactic worked. The response of American troops was to alter their movements in a way that was disproportionate to the actual threat. An analogous scenario played out following the 2001 anthrax attack in the United States in which there was a colossal disruption of postal services and massive new expenses. Yet, only 5 people died in the attack. (Compare that to the roughly 62,000 Americans who died from influenza and pneumonia that same year.)

Both of these examples serve to underscore two important points: First, biological warfare will almost certainly have a far greater psychological impact than direct impact; and second, protective measures against biological attacks are costly and inconvenient. For instance, giving soldiers vaccines against all possible biological agents would be impractical and possibly dangerous if they have been developed under emergency conditions without thorough testing. Also, vaccines have side effects. . . . Is it worth vaccinating an entire army or nation—knowing ahead of time that many will die or become sick—to protect them against an unlikely threat? From an epidemiological standpoint, the answer is clearly no, which explains why citizens do not receive smallpox vaccinations. The general rule in public health is to vaccinate only if the risk of the disease is greater than the risk of vaccination.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_9.instructions:
Directions for questions 9 to 12: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

In popular culture, Frida Kahlo stands tall. Not just as a disabled artist or a symbol of feminism but also as a figure often portrayed under Diego Rivera's shadow. Frida's vivid iconography adorns everything from tote bags to Barbie dolls. Yet, this fervour often dilutes her work into "Fridolatry," rendering her a commodified figure waiting to be "translated".

It was in 1938 that Frida painted "Lo que el agua me dio" (What the Water Gave Me). This piece, a profound reflection of her psyche and experiences, inadvertently catapulted her to international acclaim and perhaps misrepresentation. The self-portrait depicts Kahlo’s feet in a bathtub, with a surrealistic montage rising above them. French surrealist André Breton, who happened to visit Mexico during its creation, was captivated. Declaring her a "natural surrealist," he facilitated her New York debut. Though the label introduced her art to Western audiences, it was also reductive. Frida's narrative, especially in the West, shifted to that of a naive artist, untamed and instinctive, untouched by formal surrealism.

But to view Kahlo as an accidental genius is to grossly misunderstand her. She was deeply conscious of her cultural identity and astutely curated her public image. Born in Mexico City to the German-born photographer Guillermo Kahlo, Frida was well aware of her mestizo heritage. Her fashion, her unibrow accentuated with eyeliner, and even her public appearances were meticulously crafted. In a world where the personal and the political often collide, Frida stood as a symbol, embodying and manipulating her complex identity.

The dichotomy in Kahlo's global perception was also evident in her home country. Take, for instance, her and Diego's residence in Mexico City. Designed by the innovative architect Juan O’Gorman in the 1930s, the house was a beacon of modernity, embodying post-revolution ideals of simplicity and functionality. But it was more than just bricks and mortar. This house, which saw many renowned visitors, was a testament to their cosmopolitan essence, symbolizing the duality of traditional Mexican ethos and progressive internationalism.

However, such subtleties often got lost in translation. A 1934 Harper’s Bazaar feature on the couple's house focused on its folkloric elements rather than its functionalist design, perpetuating Mexico as a quaint, exotic land barely touching modernity. The narrative was clear: Mexico was the 'other', and the Rivera-Kahlo house, despite its revolutionary architecture, was another picturesque element of this otherness.

Such misinterpretations persisted. Fast forward to 2002, and Salma Hayek's portrayal of Kahlo on screen was reimagined to be more sultry, less authentic. And in a cringe-worthy 2016 concert, Madonna's interaction with a Frida lookalike from the audience reduced the artist to a mere caricature.

The reinterpretations and misappropriations of Frida's legacy raise poignant questions about how narratives, especially those of iconic figures, are shaped. Last Halloween, in an anecdotal instance, a young woman with a naturally prominent unibrow was mistakenly identified as Frida Kahlo at a party. It was a light-hearted moment, but one that underscored the problem: The essence of Kahlo, her life, her pain, her artistry, and her agency, is often eclipsed by a unibrow or a traditional Tehuana dress. The challenge then remains: How do we truly understand, appreciate, and remember Frida, beyond the stereotypes and the commodified souvenirs?
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_9.question_text: ‘In a world where the personal and the political often collide, Frida stood as a symbol, embodying and manipulating her complex identity.’ What does the author intend to convey using this statement?
options: Frida Kahlo symbolized diverse cultural, personal, and socio-political aspects and skillfully wielded her complex identity to convey her message.
While Frida Kahlo emerged as a cultural symbol, she distorted her true self, potentially misleading audiences about her genuine identity.
Frida Kahlo was able to converge her personal and political views to create a dichotomised symbol of both embodiment and manipulation.
In a realm where the political and personal intricacies of life intertwined, Frida Kahlo was able to cunningly adjust her identity for public perception.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_9.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
The statement "In a world where the personal and the political often collide, Frida stood as a symbol, embodying and manipulating her complex identity." emphasizes Frida Kahlo's capability to represent and navigate the intricacies of her personal experiences, cultural heritage, and the political contexts of her time. The term "embodying" suggests her representation of these diverse facets, while "manipulating" underscores her skill in manoeuvring or curating her identity. The author intends to convey that Kahlo was both a genuine representation of diverse facets of identity and astute in how she presented or curated that identity.

Incorrect Answers:
(2) This interpretation suggests a more deceptive or negative undertone in Kahlo's presentation of her identity, implying that she might have deliberately distorted her "true self" to mislead audiences. However, the passage paints a portrait of Kahlo as someone deeply conscious and intentional about her identity's curation. It emphasizes her astute awareness of her cultural heritage and how she showcased it. While she did manipulate her public image, this wasn't with an intent to deceive but rather to present a particular facet of her multifaceted identity. Thus, the idea of "distorting her true self" does not align with the nuanced portrayal presented in the passage.

(3) The term "dichotomised symbol" suggests a dualistic or opposing relationship between the aspects of embodiment and manipulation in Kahlo's representation. While the passage does acknowledge Kahlo's ability to both embody and manipulate her identity, it doesn't present these as dichotomous or contradictory actions. Instead, Kahlo's embodiment of her cultural, personal, and political identity seamlessly coexists with her skilful curation of that image. The essence of the passage is about Kahlo's depth and complexity, not about a dichotomy in her representation.

(4) The choice of the word "cunningly" in this option implies a sly or deceptive manipulation of Kahlo's identity. This is not the sentiment expressed in the passage. While the passage does delve into Kahlo's adeptness at curating her public image, it does so in a manner that admires her consciousness of her identity and her intent in its presentation. The term "cunningly" carries a more negative or crafty connotation, which isn't consistent with the passage's more respectful and admiring portrayal of Kahlo's actions and intentions.

options: to denote that Frida Kahlo’s legacy has been overshadowed by worshipful admiration
to highlight the commercial exploitation and commodification of the artworks of Frida Kahlo
to shed light on the dangers of reducing multifaceted Frida Kahlo to mere symbols
to lament the redution and commodification of the translated works of Frida Kahlo
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_10.solution:

(2) While the term 'Fridolatry' and the broader passage do touch upon the commercial aspects (e.g., tote bags, Barbie dolls), the author's primary grievance isn't solely about the commercial exploitation of her artworks. It's a broader concern about how Kahlo's complex identity and legacy have been reduced to mere consumable items or symbols in popular culture.

(4) The term 'Fridolatry' and the broader narrative of the passage aren't specifically concentrated on the "translated works" of Kahlo. While reduction and commodification are themes present, the main emphasis, as depicted in the passage, is on the broader spectrum of Kahlo's legacy, identity, and how these aspects are often misconstrued or oversimplified in popular representations.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_13.instructions:
Directions for questions 13 to 16: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

The wildfire that ravaged the city of Lahaina on the Hawaiian island of Maui stands out as a devastating event in the annals of American history. Its tragic consequence – claiming more lives than any other US wildfire in the preceding century – speaks volumes about the dire effects of environmental mismanagement and ecological imbalance. While poor land-use choices undeniably exacerbated the situation, the adverse impact of the unchecked feral goat population on the landscape emerged as a significant catalyst in fueling this destructive fire. Consequently, efforts towards fire suppression must prioritize the eradication of invasive species and the restoration of native flora. For Hawaii, this means intensifying control and regulation measures on its burgeoning feral ungulate population, with particular emphasis on goats.

Historical records highlight that sizable goat populations have been an integral part of Hawaii's native dry forest ecosystem for over two centuries. These creatures were first introduced to the island in 1798 as a token of appreciation for King Kamehameha I. But while they may have started as a symbol of goodwill, feral goats quickly gained notoriety for their environmental destructiveness. Their proclivity for feasting on native plants and stripping trees of their protective bark has wreaked havoc on Hawaii's indigenous species. In conjunction with the aftermath of previous fires and land-use changes attributed to the plantation industry, these voracious goats have facilitated the proliferation of invasive fire-adapted species. The cumulative effect of these factors is an ominous cycle where escalating fires create more opportunities for invasive species to thrive.

At first glance, intensive cattle grazing appears to be a viable fire-prevention measure, given its potential to reduce the abundance of these flammable invasive species. However, this approach is riddled with complications. For starters, it would necessitate ongoing management and significant water resource allocation. This would not only run counter to the global momentum towards reforestation and carbon capture but also pose threats of its own. Intense grazing, while effective at suppressing fires, could precipitate issues like soil erosion, heightening the risk of floods during the winter rainfall. Such a desolate landscape would fail miserably at supporting Hawaii's native biota, a vital biocultural treasure of the Hawaiian people.

A more holistic and sustainable strategy would be to reimagine the Hawaiian dry forest as a bulwark of biosecurity. Unlike the invasive shrubby grasslands that have come to dominate the landscape, native Hawaiian dry forests boast a dense spread of vegetation. Their multi-layered canopy and deep-rooted plant communities serve as effective barriers against soil erosion and flooding. Examples of successful initiatives, such as the ongoing efforts to rejuvenate native vegetation in cultural landmarks like Kahoolawe, underscore the indispensability of goat eradication. Shielding native species from these destructive grazers can set the stage for natural regeneration. Over time, these native species can reclaim their rightful place, gradually replacing the invasive varieties that have thrived under the goats' dominion. For lasting results in mitigating landscape-scale fires, it is imperative for communities to confine feral goats to regions removed from vital ecological zones. Concurrently, an aggressive push for landscape-scale restoration of the native Hawaiian dry forest is paramount.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_13.question_text: The author’s recommendation for mitigating landscape-scale fires is most susceptible to which one of the following?
options: It evaluates the effectiveness of a strategy based on its historical origins rather than its contemporary impact.
It infers the potential success of a solution based solely on the perceived credibility of earlier ecological interventions.
It determines the merit of a strategy based on the historical significance of feral goats rather than on the goats' current ecological impact.
It disproportionately focuses on one invasive species, potentially overlooking other significant ecological factors.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_13.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The primary recommendation the author presents centres around the eradication of invasive species, particularly focusing on feral goats, as a measure to prevent landscape-scale fires. The author attributes much of the ecological degradation to the adverse impact of feral goats on the landscape. While the role of feral goats in facilitating fire-adapted invasive species and environmental degradation is highlighted, the recommendation's heavy emphasis on them could be seen as potentially neglecting or minimizing other significant ecological factors that might contribute to wildfires.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) The passage does delve into the historical introduction of goats in Hawaii but uses their current ecological impact as the primary rationale for their removal. The author's main concern is the contemporary impact of these goats on the environment, not just their historical origins.

(2) While the passage mentions previous successful initiatives, like the efforts to rejuvenate native vegetation in places like Kahoolawe, the recommendation isn't based solely on these historical interventions. The broader argument is constructed around the current ecological role of feral goats and the benefits of native forests.

(3) Although the author provides historical context regarding the introduction of feral goats, the primary rationale for their removal is their present-day impact on the environment, facilitating the spread of fire-adapted invasive species, and directly contributing to ecological degradation. The recommendation is grounded in the goats' current ecological impact, not just their historical significance.

the native biota is both ecologically important and culturally significant to the Hawaiian people.

(2) When discussing the complications associated with intensive cattle grazing as a fire-prevention measure, the author specifically points out that such an approach would need "ongoing management and significant water resource allocation." (3) The importance of the native biota to Hawaii's ecology and culture is underlined by the author's description of it as a "vital biocultural treasure of the Hawaiian people." cl_2023_cdc_10_q_16.solution:

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_17.question_text:
The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3 and 4) below, when properly sequenced, would yield a coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper sequencing of the order of the sentences and key in the sequence of the four numbers as your answer:

1. This is the beginning of a new chapter, but the work that still lies ahead is considerable.
2. My wish is that in years to come, we won’t need to refer to such lists, and the gender of a Nobel prize winner will no longer be deemed almost more newsworthy than the merit of the work that actually earned that laureate the prize.
3. In a beautiful bit of irony, just a few hours before Goldin’s prize was announced, she published a working paper titled Why Women Won.
4. Goldin details 155 critical moments in US women’s rights history between 1905 and 2023, and then she herself became the subject of what’s certainly deserving of the 156th spot on that list.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_17.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3421
Correct Answer – 3421

Sentence 3: "In a beautiful bit of irony, just a few hours before Goldin’s prize was announced, she published a working paper titled Why Women Won." introduces the main subject of the paragraph, Goldin, her prize, and the timing of her paper's release. This sentence sets the stage for the subsequent discussion about Goldin's work and its significance.

Sentence 4 "Goldin details 155 critical moments in US women’s rights history between 1905 and 2023, and then she herself became the subject of what’s certainly deserving of the 156th spot on that list." builds on the introduction by diving deeper into the specifics of Goldin's paper and its content. It provides details on the critical moments documented in the paper and highlights the irony of Goldin becoming a part of such a list.

Sentence 2: "My wish is that in years to come, we won’t need to refer to such lists, and the gender of a Nobel prize winner will no longer be deemed almost more newsworthy than the merit of the work that actually earned that laureate the prize." transitions the discussion from Goldin's specific accomplishments and her paper to a broader perspective on gender equality. This sentence encapsulates the overarching message, emphasizing the aspiration that in the future, gender will no longer overshadow merit.

Sentence 1: "This is the beginning of a new chapter, but the work that still lies ahead is considerable." concludes the paragraph by summarizing the overall sentiment. While progress has been made, as signified by Goldin's win and her research, there remains much work to be done in the realm of gender equality and recognition.

In this sequence, the paragraph starts by highlighting Goldin's recent accomplishments, delves into the content of her paper and its significance, moves to a broader perspective on gender recognition, and concludes with a call to action and reflection on the work that lies ahead. This structure ensures a smooth and coherent flow of ideas.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_18.question_text:
There is a sentence that is missing in the paragraph below. Look at the paragraph and decide in which blank (option 1, 2, 3, or 4) the following sentence would best fit.

Sentence: This sense of detachment would allow us to see the bigger picture, rather than getting caught up in our own feelings.

Paragraph: Illeism is the practice of talking about oneself in the third person, rather than the first person. __1__. The rhetorical device is often used by politicians to try to give their words an air of objectivity. In his account of the Gallic War, for example, the emperor Julius Caesar wrote, “Caesar avenged the public” rather than “I avenged the public”. The small linguistic switch seems intended to make the statement feel a little more like historical fact, recorded by an impartial observer. To the modern ear, illeism can sound a little silly or pompous – and we may even deride famous people who choose to talk in this way. __2__. Yet recent psychological research suggests that illeism can bring some real cognitive benefits. If we are trying to make a difficult decision, speaking about ourselves in the third person can help to neutralise the emotions that could lead our thinking astray, allowing us to find a wiser solution to our problem. __3__. The idea makes intuitive sense: by switching to the third person, our descriptions of the situation will start to sound as if we are talking about someone else rather than ourselves. __4__. Whether I’m facing trials at work, conflict with my friends, or strife in the family, I find that a few moments contemplating my problems from a third-person perspective helps me to see the issue more clearly.
options: Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_18.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Correct Answer – (4)

• The paragraph starts with a definition of "illeism" and how it involves referring to oneself in the third person.
• The narrative moves to historical examples, highlighting Julius Caesar's usage to create an impression of objectivity.
• The paragraph then transitions to the modern perception of illeism, which is sometimes seen as pompous.
• Next, the psychological benefits of using illeism are discussed, focusing on how it helps neutralize emotions and aids decision-making.
• The paragraph concludes with an explanation that illeism makes it seem as if one is talking about someone else and then gives a personal account of how this perspective helps in understanding problems more clearly.

Given this flow:

Option 1 is right after introducing the concept of illeism. Placing the sentence here would disrupt the transition from definition to the historical example of Julius Caesar.

Option 2 comes after the modern-day perception of illeism. Inserting the sentence here might seem abrupt as it doesn't naturally lead into the psychological benefits.

Option 3 follows the discussion about the psychological benefits of illeism. Although this seems a potential fit, placing the sentence here might feel repetitive since the advantages of the third-person perspective have already been addressed.

Option 4 is situated right after the insight that illeism makes descriptions sound like they're about someone else. The given sentence, which emphasizes the "sense of detachment," fits perfectly here. This detachment is what allows for an objective view, acting as a bridge to the author's personal experience and validation of the benefits of such a perspective.

Considering the structure and content of the paragraph, Option 4 is indeed the most fitting location for the sentence. The idea of detachment, as provided in the given sentence, offers a smooth transition from the general understanding of illeism to the author's personal experience with its benefits.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_19.question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Contemporary psychology is a form of mythology insofar as it is an attempt to succour our need to believe in stories that provide a sense of value and signification in the context of secular modernity. Psychology is not solely the science of the mind. It’s a form of knowledge enmeshed with our mythical understanding of deeper questions of significance. In our secular age, many people no longer turn to sacred books to understand who and what they are. Psychology is where many find meaning. Indeed, the stories we tell ourselves through psychology fulfil many of the same functions served in the traditional belief practices of mythology. Falling somewhere between a social science, a natural science and a human science, it isn’t simple to determine which type of knowledge the study of the mind is supposed to pursue. Psychology aspires to the status of the physical sciences, but it tries to explain much more, and ends up revealing much less.
options: Psychology is the modern mythology that has replaced the erstwhile sacred books by providing a sense of value and significance to modern humans.
In today's secular world, where religion no longer holds value, psychology provides a sense of value, yet it still fails by revealing very little.
Psychology is an amalgamation of mythology, social science, natural science and human science that attempts to provide worth in the context of modern secular society.
Contemporary psychology offers frameworks reminiscent of past mythologies to interpret the human experience, yet it may fall short in its endeavour.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_19.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The passage emphasizes the role of contemporary psychology as a source of understanding and meaning, likening it to the role previously played by mythologies. Just as myths provided narratives and explanations for human experiences in bygone eras, psychology tries to fill that gap today. The passage also touches upon the complexities and limitations of psychology, especially when it compares psychology's aspirations to the status of physical sciences and how it might not fully meet those aspirations. Option (4) captures both these themes effectively — the comparison to past mythologies and the potential shortcomings of psychology.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) While the passage does liken psychology to mythology in its function of offering meaning, it doesn't exactly state that psychology has "replaced" sacred books. The word "replaced" suggests a total supplanting, whereas the passage indicates a shift or transition in which people find meaning.

(2) This option misrepresents the passage in two key ways. Firstly, the passage doesn’t imply that “religion no longer holds value”; it simply states that many no longer turn to sacred books for understanding. Secondly, while the passage does discuss psychology's limitations, the word "fails" might be too strong a characterization.

(3) This option focuses heavily on the multi-faceted nature of psychology as being between various sciences. While this aspect is mentioned in the passage, the central theme of the passage revolves around psychology's role in providing value and meaning in a manner akin to mythologies. This option doesn't capture the essence of the comparison to mythology as effectively as it should.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_20.question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

The self-serving bias is the tendency of people to seek out information and use it in ways that advance their self-interest. In other words, people often unconsciously make decisions that serve themselves in ways that other people might view as indefensible or unethical. Studies show that we can easily see how the self-serving bias affects others’ actions, but we have difficulty realizing how it affects our own. For example, doctors tend to believe that they are immune from the influence of gifts they receive from pharmaceutical companies. But, studies show those gifts have a significant effect on what medications doctors prescribe. One study found that 64% of doctors believed that the freebies they received from pharmaceutical companies influenced other doctors. However, only 16% of doctors thought it affected their own actions. So, the self-serving bias often blinds us to the ways in which we are prejudiced in favour of ourselves. Indeed, it can cause even the most well-intentioned of us to completely overlook our own bad actions.
options: The self-serving bias skews judgment, as is evident when doctors falsely believe gifts influence others but not themselves.
The self-serving bias blinds us to our own prejudices, often making us overlook our missteps while easily spotting them in others.
The significant danger of the self-serving bias lies in its potential to impede objective decision-making and even lead to unethical choices.
The self-serving bias is a cognitive bias that leads people to consciously perceive and interpret information in a way that benefits or favours themselves.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_20.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The passage highlights the nature of the self-serving bias, emphasizing how it can obscure our self-awareness of our own actions and biases, yet allows us to recognize those same biases in others. The passage uses the example of doctors receiving gifts from pharmaceutical companies to illustrate this. The majority of doctors believed others were influenced by such gifts, but only a minority believed they themselves were influenced. This central theme of the self-serving bias preventing self-awareness is captured accurately in option (2).

Incorrect Answers:
(1) While this option correctly identifies the example of doctors from the passage, it narrows down the essence of the passage to this singular example. The broader point the passage is making isn't solely about skewed judgment but about a lack of self-awareness of one’s own biases.

(3) The passage does discuss the idea that the self-serving bias can lead individuals to make decisions that others might find "indefensible or unethical." However, this option's focus on the "danger" and the potential to impede "objective decision-making" is more of an extrapolation than a direct representation of the passage's core idea.

(4) This option misrepresents the nature of the self-serving bias, as discussed in the passage. The passage highlights the "unconscious" nature of the self-serving bias. The use of "consciously" in this option is inaccurate and deviates from the passage’s main point.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_21.question_text:
The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3 and 4) below, when properly sequenced, would yield a coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper sequencing of the order of the sentences and key in the sequence of the four numbers as your answer:

1. Kerri True, a psychology professor, highlighted that adding plausible details to fabrications increases the chances of confusing their origins later.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_24.question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

The Japanese term kokugaku has various English translations, such as ‘Japan Studies’ and ‘National Learning’, making it challenging to define. In the seventeenth century, Japan experienced significant growth in scholarship, influenced by peace, prosperity, and literacy, leading to increased studies of Japanese history, literature, and traditions, which can broadly be classified as kokugaku. However, in the eighteenth century, the narrow sense of kokugaku emphasized Japanese uniqueness, contrasting primarily with Chinese influences. This focus on Japanese identity evolved during the nineteenth century, extending its frame of reference to include Europe. During the Tokugawa period (1600–1868), Kokugaku was recognized through the works of scholars like Keichū and Norinaga. Post the 1868 Meiji Restoration, Kokugaku played an ideological role, influencing empire creation and evolving into fields like folklore. By the late 20th century, the term's use had shifted, with modern-day nativist studies often referred to as Nihonjinron.
options: Over centuries, kokugaku transitioned from a study of Japanese traditions to an emphasis on national identity, contrasting with Chinese and European elements, eventually forgotten and replaced with Nihonjinron by the 20th century.

Tracing the evolution of kokugaku, it began as an exploration of Japanese culture in the 17th century, contrasted with foreign influences in subsequent centuries, and by the 20th century, merged with the concept of Nihonjinron.

From its 17th-century roots studying Japanese history and literature to its influences on Chinese literature and its modern connotation as Nihonjinron, kokugaku has been a dynamic reflection of Japan's evolving identity.

Kokugaku's journey, starting as an in-depth study of Japanese culture in the 17th century, evolved through various phases of struggles with foreign countries. It finally found its modern expression in Nihonjinron by the 20th century.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_24.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Correct Answer – (2)
The term 'kokugaku' went through various evolutionary stages. Beginning in the 17th century with a focus on Japanese culture, it later defined itself in contrast to external influences (like Chinese and European thought). By the 20th century, 'kokugaku' transitioned in its significance, with the term 'Nihonjinron' taking prominence in describing nativist studies. (2) efficiently captures this progression, noting the origin of 'kokugaku', its positioning against foreign influences, and its eventual confluence with the concept of Nihonjinron.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) This option does track the evolution of 'kokugaku', starting from its roots in Japanese traditions and its shifting focus to a more national identity. However, the assertion that kokugaku was "eventually forgotten" doesn't align with the passage's content. The term's use might have shifted, but it wasn't forgotten.

(3) This option accurately captures the trajectory of 'kokugaku' from its origins focused on Japanese culture to its present association with Nihonjinron, emphasizing its role in reflecting Japan's identity over time. However, the problem with this option is the misrepresentation that kokugaku had an "influence on Chinese literature," which isn't stated or implied in the passage.

(4) The phrase "struggles with foreign countries" adds an external dimension that the passage doesn't emphasize. The passage discusses internal shifts in thought and contrasts with foreign influences, not necessarily struggles with foreign nations.

(vii) Every child received at least two types of assets.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_45.question_text: The average weight of 45 children is 8 kg. Out of these 45 children, exactly 30 have weights that are 8 kg or less. If all the children’s weights are in integral kilograms, what is the highest possible value for the average weight (in kg) of these 30 children?
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_45.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
In order to keep the average weight of the 30 children as high as possible, we need to keep the average weight of the 15 children above 8 kg as low as possible. Since the weights of all children are in integral kilograms, the least value that the 15 children above 8 kg can take is 9 kg.
So, the sum of the weights of 15 children = 15 × 9 = 135 kg
So the sum of the weights of the remaining 30 children = 8 × 45 – 135 = 225 kg
Hence, the average weight of the remaining 30 children is 225/30 = 7.5 kg.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_47.question_text: ABCD is an isosceles trapezium that is drawn in a coordinate plane. AB and CD are the sides of the trapezium parallel to the X-axis. Find the minimum number of lattice points inside the trapezium.
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_47.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1

If AB, CD are parallel (whose height is 5) and both the equal sides BC, AD have equal number (which is 6) of lattice points, then it is possible only if AB || CD || X-axis and BC, AD make a 45° angle with the X-axis.
So the least number of lattice points inside the trapezium would be when say AB = 1 and CD = 11
Hence, the number of lattice points inside would be 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 = 20.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_52.solution:

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_56.question_text: In a garden, there are three types of flower pots: roses, tulips, and daisies. If there are 315 flower pots of daisies in the garden, how many flower pots of roses are there?
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_56.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 216
Let number of flower pots of roses, tulips, and daisies be r, t, and d respectively.
So, r : t = 3 : 5, t : d = 8 : 7
So, ratio of r : t : d = 24 : 40 : 35
Since there are 35 flower pots of daisies in the garden, therefore, 35k = 315 gives, k = 9.
Hence, number of flower pots of roses are 24k = 24 × 9 = 216.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_61.question_text: A tank has two pipes A and B both of which can fill and also drain. For both the pipes the rate of filling is same as the rate of draining. Both pipes together take ‘t’ minutes to fill the tank and A can fill 3/4th of the tank in ‘t’ minutes. If the tank is empty and pipe A is used to fill the tank while B is used to empty it, then what percent of the tank will be empty after ‘t/5’ minutes?
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_61.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 90
Let the time taken to fill the tank when both pipes are filling it be t minutes.
Then, the time taken for both pipes together to empty the tank will also be t minutes.
It is given that in t minutes pipe A alone fills 3/4th of the tank.
Hence, in t minutes pipe B can fill 1/4th of the tank.
Now, it also follows that pipe A alone can empty 3/4th of the tank in ‘t’ minutes and pipe B alone would empty 1/4th of the tank in ‘t’ minutes (when they are used to empty the tank individually). If pipe A is used to fill and pipe B is used to empty for t/5 minutes, then the tank will be = (1/5) (3/4 – 1/4) = 1/10 full = 10% full. Hence, the tank will be 90% empty.

cl_2023_cdc_10_q_65.question_text: Jeevan needs to reach a destination 84 km downstream on a river. He can row at 14 km/h in still water. The river flows at a usual rate of 2 km/h but due to tides every two hours, the speed doubles and then after another two hours it halves. If Jeevan starts at 1:00 PM at the change of tide when the river begins to flow at 2 km/h, then at what time will he reach his destination?
cl_2023_cdc_10_q_65.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The distance covered in the first 2 hours (starting with the usual rate) = 2 × (2 + 14) km = 32 km.
In the next 2 hours the speed of the current doubles and the distance traveled in those 2 hours = 2 (2 × 2 + 14) = 36 km.
So, every 4 hours, distance traveled = 32 + 36 = 68 km.
Now, 84 = 68 + 16; Jeevan covers 68 km in 4 hours and the next 16 km in 1 hour. Total time taken = 5 hours
Hence, Jeevan will reach his destination at 1:00 + 5:00 = 6:00 PM.
